text:
Property and Land Disputes in Uttar Pradesh — Overview

Property disputes in UP fall under several laws depending on the type of dispute. Understanding which law applies is the first step.

For agricultural land and village land: The UP Revenue Code 2006 and the UP Zamindari Abolition & Land Reforms Act 1950 govern all land records, mutation, encroachment, and tenure rights. The basic land documents are: Khatauni (Record of Rights — who owns which plot), Khasra (field-level plot details), and Bhu-Naksha (boundary map). All of these are now available online at upbhulekh.gov.in for free.

For residential/commercial property and builder projects: The Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act 2016 (RERA) protects buyers. UP RERA was launched on October 27, 2016 at up-rera.in. Every builder project above 500 sq.m. or 8 units must be RERA-registered. Buyers can file complaints at up-rera.in for builder fraud, delay, or defects.

For all property sales: The Transfer of Property Act 1882 and the Registration Act 1908 govern how property changes hands. Every sale of immovable property above Rs. 100 must be done through a registered Sale Deed at the Sub-Registrar's office. An unregistered agreement gives no legal ownership.

For property in someone else's name (benami): The Benami Transactions (Prohibition) Act 1988 (amended 2016) applies. Benami property can be confiscated by the Central Government, and the person who does benami transactions faces 1–7 years rigorous imprisonment.

For stamp duty in UP: Stamp duty is 7% of market value for men, 6% for women (on properties up to Rs. 10 lakh). For transfers among blood relatives — flat Rs. 5,000 stamp duty under the 2024 amendment. Registration fee is 1% of property value. Pay at igrsup.gov.in.

metadata.source: UP Revenue Code 2006, RERA 2016, Registration Act 1908, Benami Act 1988

text:
How to Check Land Records Online in UP — Khatauni, Khasra, and Bhu-Naksha

Before buying any land or taking any legal action over a land dispute, always check land records online first. This protects you from fraud and strengthens your case.

UP Bhulekh Portal (upbhulekh.gov.in) — Official government portal maintained by the Board of Revenue, UP (contact: 0522-2217145, bhulekh-up@gov.in). It is free to view. Available 24 hours.

What you can check on UP Bhulekh:
1. Real-Time Khatauni (Record of Rights) — shows registered owner's name, Khasra/plot numbers, area in hectares, tenure type (bhumidhar/asami), and revenue payable. This is legally valid and accepted by banks for loans and courts as evidence.
2. Khasra details — plot-level information including land use and survey details.
3. Dispute status — check if the plot is involved in any pending legal dispute. If yes, do NOT buy until the dispute is resolved.
4. Sale status — check if the plot has any sale restriction. A sale-restricted plot cannot be legally transferred.
5. Bhu-Naksha (boundary map) — digital cadastral map at upbhunaksha.gov.in showing exact plot boundaries. Essential for boundary disputes.

How to search:
Step 1: Visit upbhulekh.gov.in
Step 2: Click 'Khatauni (Adhikar Abhilekh) ki Nakal Dekhein'
Step 3: Select District → Tehsil → Village from dropdowns
Step 4: Search by Khasra number, Khata number, or owner name
Step 5: View and download the Real-Time Khatauni

For certified copies needed in court: Visit the Tahsildar's office and pay the prescribed fee. Online copies are valid for reference but certified copies may be required for formal court submissions.

If records show errors: File a correction application at the Tahsildar's office under Section 32 of the UP Revenue Code 2006. SDO/Tahsildar/Revenue Inspector can correct errors in Khatauni and Khasra records.

metadata.source: UP Revenue Code 2006 S.30, S.31, S.32, S.221; upbhulekh.gov.in

text:
Mutation (Dakhil Kharij) — Getting Land Transferred in Your Name in UP

Mutation is the process of updating the Khatauni (Record of Rights) to reflect the new owner's name after a property transfer. It is NOT the same as ownership — ownership comes from a registered Sale Deed. But mutation is essential for: applying for bank loans against the land, accessing government schemes, and as supporting evidence in disputes.

When is mutation required?
- After purchase of land by registered Sale Deed
- After inheriting land on death of the previous owner (succession mutation)
- After receiving land as a gift (registered Gift Deed required)
- After a court decree or partition order

Mutation process under UP Revenue Code 2006, Sections 33–35:
Step 1: Report the transfer to the Tahsildar of the area where the land is located — submit a written application with your registered Sale Deed / Succession Certificate / Gift Deed copy.
Step 2: The Tahsildar registers your report and issues a public proclamation inviting objections.
Step 3: If no objection is raised within the specified period and the case is undisputed, the Tahsildar directly directs the Khatauni to be updated in your name — Section 35(1)(a).
Step 4: If there is a dispute, the Tahsildar holds an inquiry, hears both parties, decides the dispute, and then updates the Khatauni accordingly.
Step 5: The Tahsildar's order can be appealed to the Sub-Divisional Officer (SDO) within 30 days — Section 35(2).

Fee: The State Government prescribes a fee for entry of mutation based on the transfer value.

Note: For property registered through the Sub-Registrar's office, the Registrar sends an automatic intimation to the Tahsildar — Section 36, UP Revenue Code 2006. But it is still advisable to separately submit the mutation application to avoid delays.

Property Registration and Stamp Duty in UP — What You Must Know

Every sale of immovable property above Rs. 100 must be registered at the Sub-Registrar's office. This is mandatory under Section 17 of the Registration Act 1908. An unregistered deed is legally invalid — Section 49 of the same Act states that an unregistered document cannot be produced as evidence in court for any right relating to immovable property.

Key rule from Supreme Court (Suraj Lamp v. State of Haryana, 2011): Power of Attorney transactions do NOT transfer ownership. Many people try to sell land through Power of Attorney to avoid stamp duty — this is illegal and the buyer gets no valid ownership.

Stamp duty rates in UP (2025):
- Male buyer: 7% of property's market value
- Female buyer: 6% of property's market value (for properties up to Rs. 10 lakh; above Rs. 10 lakh — same 7%)
- Registration fee: 1% of property value (payable separately)
- Transfer between blood relatives (sale, gift, partition): Flat Rs. 5,000 stamp duty + Rs. 1,000 processing fee — UP Stamp Amendment 2024
- Partition deed within family: Maximum Rs. 5,000 stamp duty — UP Cabinet decision September 2024

Where to pay: igrsup.gov.in — online stamp duty payment is available. You can also pay at the Sub-Registrar's office.

Registration process:
Step 1: Prepare Sale Deed with a lawyer
Step 2: Pay stamp duty (online or at bank)
Step 3: Both buyer and seller appear at Sub-Registrar's office with ID proof (Aadhaar, PAN), photographs, and fingerprints
Step 4: Sub-Registrar registers the deed and gives a registered copy
Step 5: After registration, apply for mutation at the Tahsildar's office

Deadline: Document must be presented for registration within 4 months of execution — Registration Act S.23. If missed, you can present it within a further 4 months on payment of fine, with Sub-Registrar's permission.

metadata.source: Registration Act 1908 S.17, S.23, S.49; Transfer of Property Act 1882 S.54; UP Stamp Amendment 2024

text:
Your Rights as a Landowner and Property Buyer in UP

As a landowner (bhumidhar) in UP:
1. Right to possess, use, and cultivate your land. Bhumidhar with transferable rights can also sell, mortgage, exchange, or gift the land.
2. Right to inspect land records free online at upbhulekh.gov.in and get certified copies from the Tahsildar — UP Revenue Code 2006, Section 221.
3. Right to mutation — to have Khatauni updated in your name after purchase or inheritance — Section 35.
4. Right against encroachment — approach SDO under Section 23; SDO must act within 3 months.
5. Right to succession — on death of a bhumidhar, land passes to legal heirs. Apply for succession mutation at Tahsildar's office.
6. Right to appeal — Tahsildar's order can be appealed to SDO (30 days), then Collector (30 days), then Commissioner (30 days), then Board of Revenue (90 days, on questions of law).

As a homebuyer (flat/plot from a builder) under RERA 2016:
1. Right to verify RERA registration before buying — check at up-rera.in.
2. Right to full information — builder must display sanctioned plans, layout, approvals, and quarterly status updates on the RERA website.
3. Right against excess advance — builder cannot take more than 10% before signing a registered Agreement for Sale — Section 13.
4. Right to the agreed flat — builder cannot change sanctioned plans without your written consent — Section 14.
5. Right to 5-year structural defect warranty — any defect reported within 5 years must be fixed free within 30 days — Section 14(3).
6. Right to possession or refund — if builder delays, you can choose: (a) stay in project + get interest for every month of delay, OR (b) withdraw and get full refund with interest from date of each payment — Section 18.
7. Right to file complaint at up-rera.in with Rs. 1,000 fee — Section 31.
8. Right to check if 70% of collected funds are in a dedicated escrow account — builder must maintain this — Section 4(2)(l)(D).

metadata.source: UP Revenue Code 2006 S.221, S.35, S.23; RERA 2016 S.13, S.14, S.18, S.31

text:
Encroachment and Boundary Disputes — How to Resolve in UP

Encroachment means when someone illegally occupies or builds on your land. Boundary disputes arise when neighbors disagree on where one plot ends and another begins. Both are very common in rural UP and have specific remedies.

Step 1 — Gather your documents:
Before approaching any authority, collect: Real-Time Khatauni from upbhulekh.gov.in (showing your name as owner), Bhu-Naksha (boundary map from upbhunaksha.gov.in), and your registered Sale Deed or Succession Certificate.

Step 2 — File complaint with SDO / Tahsildar:
For boundary disputes or illegal dispossession, approach the Sub-Divisional Officer (SDO) under Section 23, UP Revenue Code 2006. The SDO will: conduct a summary inquiry, identify the rightful holder based on records, and put them in possession using force if necessary. All proceedings must be concluded within 3 months.

Step 3 — Appeal if SDO order is unfavorable:
Appeal to the Commissioner within 30 days of the SDO's order. The Commissioner's order is final at the revenue court level.

For encroachment on public/common land (village roads, chak roads, common land):
File a complaint with the Tahsildar. The encroacher is liable to a fine of Rs. 1,000–10,000 under Section 227 of the UP Revenue Code. The SDO can also issue a personal bond of up to Rs. 15,000 for future abstinence.

For destruction of boundary marks: Separate penal action available — damages payable plus criminal prosecution.

For land mafia or large-scale encroachment: File an FIR at the police station + approach the District Magistrate (DM). Also complain on CM Helpline 1076 for government intervention. In serious cases, approach the Allahabad High Court for a writ of mandamus directing action.

Do NOT take the law into your own hands: Forcibly removing an encroachment yourself can lead to a counter-case being filed against you. Follow the legal process.

metadata.source: UP Revenue Code 2006 S.23, S.227; UP Zamindari Abolition Act 1950 S.202-207

text:
Filing a RERA Complaint Against Your Builder in UP — Complete Guide

UP RERA (Uttar Pradesh Real Estate Regulatory Authority) handles disputes between homebuyers and builders. UP RERA handles about 40% of all RERA complaints in India, making it one of the most active authorities. Launched October 27, 2016.

Who can complain: Any person who has booked, purchased, or is in any transaction with a builder for a RERA-registered project.

Before filing: Verify the project is RERA-registered at up-rera.in. If not registered, the builder has already violated RERA Section 3 and you can complain about that too.

Step-by-step complaint process:
Step 1: Visit up-rera.in → click 'Complaints'
Step 2: A prompt asks if you want conciliation with the builder — choose 'Yes' for settlement attempt, 'No' to proceed directly to formal complaint
Step 3: Answer if there is a previous RERA order on this matter — select appropriately
Step 4: Answer if there is a pending court case — if yes, you may not be able to file RERA complaint simultaneously
Step 5: Pay Rs. 1,000 fee online — you receive a complaint number on successful payment
Step 6: Upload: allotment letter, Builder Buyer Agreement (BBA), payment receipts, and correspondence with builder
Step 7: After online filing, submit self-attested physical copies of complaint form and all documents to the UP RERA office in Lucknow or Greater Noida (NCR Bench) — mandatory as per April 2024 guidelines
Step 8: UP RERA legal division scrutinizes complaint within 7 days and communicates objections within 15 days for rectification
Step 9: After rectification, complaint is marked 'Fit for Hearing' and scheduled before a bench

What RERA can order: Possession of flat with interest for delay. Refund of entire amount with interest. Compensation for defects. Cancellation of builder's RERA registration. Penalty on builder up to 5% of project cost per day for non-compliance.

If dissatisfied with RERA order: Appeal to UP Real Estate Appellate Tribunal within 60 days. Builder must deposit at least 30% of penalty before appeal is heard — RERA S.43(5). Further appeal to Allahabad High Court within 60 days.

metadata.source: RERA 2016 S.31, S.43, S.44, S.58; UP RERA Guidelines April 2024

text:
Benami Property — What Is It, Risks, and What Is NOT Benami

The Benami Transactions (Prohibition) Act 1988, amended significantly in 2016, prohibits buying property in someone else's name to hide ownership or avoid taxes.

What is a benami transaction (Section 2(9)):
A property is benami when: (a) It is held in one person's name (benamidar) but paid for by another person (beneficial owner) for their own benefit. (b) Property is held in a fictitious name. (c) The actual owner denies knowledge of owning the property. (d) The person who paid for it cannot be traced.

Consequences of benami:
- Benami property is confiscated by the Central Government — Section 5
- The real owner LOSES all rights to recover the property — Section 4
- No compensation is paid on confiscation — Section 27(3)
- Criminal penalty: 1–7 years rigorous imprisonment + fine up to 25% of the property's fair market value — Section 53
- Providing false information: 6 months–5 years imprisonment + 10% of property value — Section 54

What is NOT benami (legal exemptions):
1. Property held by Karta (head) of a Hindu Undivided Family (HUF) for family benefit from known HUF sources — NOT benami.
2. Property in wife's or child's name from your declared income/known sources — NOT benami. This is a very common and legal arrangement in India.
3. Property in name of brother, sister, or lineal ancestor/descendant where both names appear as joint owners from known sources — NOT benami.
4. Trustee, executor, director, or depository holding property in fiduciary capacity — NOT benami.

How benami is enforced: An Initiating Officer (Income Tax Department) issues notice, provisionally attaches the property for 90 days, and refers the case to the Adjudicating Authority. The Adjudicating Authority then passes an order — if benami is confirmed, the property is confiscated.

For UP citizens: Report suspected benami property to the Income Tax Department (helpline: 1800-103-0025). The Initiating Officer takes action.

metadata.source: Benami Transactions (Prohibition) Act 1988 (amended 2016) S.2(9), S.3, S.4, S.5, S.24, S.27, S.53, S.54

text:
Bhumidhar Rights in UP — Understanding Your Land Tenure

After the zamindari system was abolished by the UP Zamindari Abolition & Land Reforms Act 1950, new land tenure categories were created. The most important for ordinary citizens is Bhumidhar.

What is Bhumidhar:
Bhumidhar is the highest category of land tenure in UP. A bhumidhar has permanent, heritable rights over agricultural land. There are two types:

1. Bhumidhar with Transferable Rights: Can sell, mortgage, exchange, or gift land freely. This is the most common land tenure in UP villages. Land passes to legal heirs on death.

2. Bhumidhar with Non-Transferable Rights: Cannot sell or transfer land — only inheritance is allowed. Mostly applies to land allotted under government welfare schemes. This land cannot be sold even voluntarily.

How to become a bhumidhar: Most cultivators were automatically made bhumidhar when the Act came into force in 1950. Since then, bhumidhar status passes through inheritance or legal transfer.

Bhumidhar's duties:
- Must pay land revenue (bhumi kar) to the government
- Must report transfers to the Tahsildar for mutation
- Must maintain the land and not abandon it

Inheritance of bhumidhar rights:
On death of a bhumidhar, rights pass to legal heirs. The heirs must:
1. Apply for succession mutation at the Tahsildar's office — UP Revenue Code 2006, Section 33
2. Submit death certificate of previous owner + relationship proof (legal heir certificate)
3. Tahsildar updates the Khatauni

If there is a dispute about succession:
1. Tahsildar holds a formal inquiry
2. Appeal to SDO within 30 days
3. Can proceed to civil court for title declaration if revenue remedy is inadequate

Land ceiling: Maximum land holding per family is limited under the UP Imposition of Ceiling on Land Holdings Act 1960. Excess land was acquired by the government and distributed to landless labourers.

metadata.source: UP Zamindari Abolition & Land Reforms Act 1950; UP Revenue Code 2006 S.33

text:
How Property is Legally Transferred in India — Sale, Gift, Mortgage, and Lease

The Transfer of Property Act 1882 governs how immovable property changes hands. Understanding these modes prevents disputes.

Sale (Section 54, Transfer of Property Act 1882):
A sale is the transfer of ownership in exchange for money. For immovable property above Rs. 100: Must be done through a registered Sale Deed. The seller must deliver physical possession. Ownership transfers only on registration — NOT on payment of money alone. An agreement to sell (bayanama) does NOT transfer ownership — it is only a contract. After payment and registration, apply for mutation at the Tahsildar's office.

Gift (Section 122, 123, Transfer of Property Act 1882):
A gift is voluntary transfer without consideration (no money). For immovable property: Must be through a registered Gift Deed. Gift must be accepted by the receiver during the donor's lifetime. In UP: stamp duty of Rs. 5,000 for family gifts (2024 amendment). Gift Deed must be presented for registration within 4 months of execution.

Mortgage (Section 58, Transfer of Property Act 1882):
Mortgage is using property as security for a loan. Six types exist: simple mortgage, mortgage by deposit of title deeds (equitable — very common for bank loans, does not require registration), English mortgage, usufructuary mortgage, mortgage by conditional sale, and anomalous mortgage. After repaying the loan fully, the mortgagor has the right to get the property released — right of redemption (Section 60). If borrower defaults, lender can take legal action for foreclosure (Section 67).

Lease (Section 105, Transfer of Property Act 1882):
Lease is temporary transfer of property right for rent. Leases above 1 year must be registered. Lease of 1 year or less can be oral or written without registration.

Partition (family settlement):
When co-owners divide jointly held property. Must be through a registered Partition Deed. In UP: stamp duty on partition deeds capped at Rs. 5,000 (UP Cabinet decision, September 2024). After partition, each party applies for separate mutation.

metadata.source: Transfer of Property Act 1882 S.54, S.58, S.60, S.67, S.105, S.122, S.123; Registration Act 1908 S.17

text:
Time Limits and Deadlines for Property Disputes in UP

Missing a deadline in property law can permanently destroy your right to remedy. Here are the most important ones:

For Mutation:
- No strict deadline to apply for mutation, but apply within 3 months of transfer to avoid disputes
- Appeal Tahsildar's mutation order to SDO: within 30 days — UP Revenue Code 2006, Section 35(2)
- Second appeal to Board of Revenue: within 90 days from first appeal order — Section 208

For Boundary/Encroachment:
- SDO must conclude summary inquiry within 3 months of application — Section 23(3), UP Revenue Code
- Appeal SDO's order to Commissioner: within 30 days
- Commissioner's order is final at revenue level — no further appeal in revenue courts

For Property Registration:
- Must present document for registration within 4 months of execution — Registration Act 1908, Section 23
- If missed: can apply within 4 more months with SDO/District Registrar's permission and fine payment
- Documents presented after 8 months CANNOT be registered under any circumstance

For RERA Complaints:
- File RERA complaint as soon as the violation occurs — no strict limitation period but delays weaken the case
- RERA must dispose of complaint within 60 days — RERA Section 29(4)
- Appeal RERA Authority's order to Appellate Tribunal: within 60 days — RERA Section 44(2)
- Appeal Tribunal's order to High Court: within 60 days — RERA Section 58
- Structural defect warranty: report within 5 years of possession — RERA Section 14(3)

For Benami:
- Provisional attachment: 90 days from notice — Benami Act Section 24(3)
- Adjudication order must be passed within 1 year from reference — Benami Act Section 26(7)
- Appeal Adjudicating Authority's order: within 45 days — Benami Act Section 46(1)
- Appeal Appellate Tribunal's order to High Court: within 60 days — Benami Act Section 49

For civil court cases (title disputes): General limitation under Limitation Act 1963 — suit for possession of immovable property: 12 years from date of dispossession.

metadata.source: UP Revenue Code 2006 S.23, S.35, S.207, S.208; RERA 2016 S.44, S.58; Registration Act 1908 S.23; Benami Act S.24, S.26, S.46, S.49

text:
Penalties — What Builders, Encroachers, and Fraudsters Face Under Property Laws

Under RERA 2016 (for builder violations):
- Project not registered with RERA (Section 59): Fine up to 10% of project cost. Continued violation: up to 3 years imprisonment + further 10% fine, or both.
- False information given during RERA registration (Section 60): Fine up to 5% of project cost.
- Non-compliance with RERA Authority's orders (Section 63): Fine up to 5% of project cost per day of continued default — cumulative.
- Non-compliance with Appellate Tribunal's order (Section 64): Up to 3 years imprisonment + fine up to 10% of project cost, or both.
- Real estate agent operating without registration (Section 62): Rs. 10,000 per day, up to 5% of property cost.

Under UP Revenue Code 2006 (for land violations):
- Encroachment on public road, common land, or village path (Section 227): Fine Rs. 1,000–10,000 minimum. Personal bond up to Rs. 15,000 for future abstinence from encroachment.
- Destruction or removal of boundary marks: Damages payable to affected party + criminal prosecution.
- Non-compliance with SDO / Tahsildar orders: Can result in summary eviction using police force.

Under Benami Transactions Act 1988 (amended 2016):
- Entering into a benami transaction to defeat law, avoid statutory dues, or defraud creditors (Section 53): 1–7 years rigorous imprisonment + fine up to 25% of fair market value of property.
- Providing false information to Benami authorities (Section 54): 6 months–5 years rigorous imprisonment + fine up to 10% of fair market value.
- The benami property itself is confiscated — no compensation paid to the real owner.

Under Transfer of Property Act 1882 / BNS 2023:
- Fraudulent transfer of property to defeat creditors (BNS Section 320): Transfer set aside by court + criminal prosecution.
- Selling the same property to two different buyers (double sale): Criminal fraud charges + the buyer who registers first gets priority.

Under Registration Act 1908:
- Executing property documents without registration: Document becomes inadmissible in court — effectively worthless.
- Penalty for delayed registration: Fine plus interest on stamp duty shortfall.

metadata.source: RERA 2016 S.59-68; UP Revenue Code 2006 S.227; Benami Act S.53, S.54; Registration Act 1908 S.49; BNS 2023 S.320

text:
Where to Go When Authorities Do Not Act — Escalation in Property Disputes

Revenue court hierarchy for land disputes:
Level 1: Patwari / Revenue Inspector — for day-to-day land record updates and minor corrections
Level 2: Tahsildar — for mutation, record correction, small land disputes. Must conclude mutation within reasonable time.
Level 3: Sub-Divisional Officer (SDO) — for boundary disputes, encroachment, and appeal of Tahsildar's orders. Must resolve in 3 months.
Level 4: Collector (District) — for appeal of certain orders
Level 5: Commissioner (Division) — for appeal of SDO boundary orders; final at this level for many matters
Level 6: Board of Revenue (UP) — highest revenue court. Hears second appeals on substantial questions of law within 90 days.

For RERA disputes:
Level 1: UP RERA Authority — file complaint at up-rera.in. Bench decides within 60 days.
Level 2: UP Real Estate Appellate Tribunal — appeal within 60 days. Builder must deposit 30% of penalty first.
Level 3: Allahabad High Court — further appeal within 60 days on questions of law.

When to approach civil court:
For title disputes that are too complex for revenue courts (e.g., forged documents, fraud, adverse possession claims, disputes between co-owners with complex family history). Suit for possession of immovable property: 12-year limitation under Limitation Act 1963.

For criminal matters (land fraud, document forgery, encroachment with violence):
File FIR at police station under BNS sections for fraud, cheating, forgery. Forgery of property documents is punishable under BNS Section 336. Also approach District Magistrate.

When system fails — escalation options:
- CM Helpline 1076 — for government official inaction
- National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) — for rights violations
- Allahabad High Court writ petition — when all other remedies fail
- Free legal aid: UP Legal Services Authority (0522-2209457) or NALSA (15100)

metadata.source: UP Revenue Code 2006 S.207, S.208, S.23; RERA 2016 S.43, S.44, S.58

text:
Frequently Asked Questions — Property and Land Disputes in UP

Q: I bought land 5 years ago but never got mutation done — is my ownership still valid?
A: Your ownership is valid as long as you have the registered Sale Deed from the Sub-Registrar's office. Mutation only updates the revenue record — it does not determine ownership. However, you should get mutation done immediately to avoid complications in future transactions, loans, and disputes. Apply at the Tahsildar's office with your Sale Deed copy.

Q: The person I bought land from has died — can his legal heirs claim the land back?
A: No. A registered Sale Deed is binding and transfers ownership permanently. The seller's heirs cannot reclaim land that was properly sold through a registered deed. The heirs can only inherit whatever the deceased actually owned at the time of death. Ensure you get mutation done in your name promptly.

Q: My builder promised a swimming pool and club house but is now saying the project is changed — what can I do?
A: Under RERA Section 14, the builder cannot change sanctioned plans or specifications agreed with you without your written consent. For changes to common areas (like club house, swimming pool), written consent of two-thirds of all allottees is required. File a complaint at up-rera.in demanding either delivery of promised amenities or compensation.

Q: The seller showed me a Power of Attorney (PoA) from the original owner — can I buy land based on this?
A: Be very cautious. The Supreme Court held in Suraj Lamp & Industries v. State of Haryana (2011) that Power of Attorney transactions do NOT transfer ownership of immovable property. Only a registered Sale Deed signed by the actual owner transfers ownership. If the original owner is alive, insist that they sign the Sale Deed personally. PoA-based sales are risky and have been misused extensively for fraud.

Q: Someone is farming on my land and says he has an old khatauni in his name — what should I do?
A: Get the current Real-Time Khatauni from upbhulekh.gov.in. If your name is there, file a complaint with the SDO for restoration of possession under Section 23, UP Revenue Code. If the old khatauni is from before your registered purchase, the registered Sale Deed gives you superior title. If the Khatauni shows his name, check how the dispute arose and consult a lawyer.

Q: Can I check if a plot in a new township/colony is RERA-registered before buying?
A: Yes. Visit up-rera.in and search for the project by name or developer name. If it is registered, you will see the registration number, project details, approvals, completion timeline, and any complaints filed. Never give more than 10% advance to any builder before seeing a valid RERA registration number and signing a registered Agreement for Sale.

metadata.source: UP Revenue Code 2006; RERA 2016; Transfer of Property Act 1882 S.54; Supreme Court — Suraj Lamp 2011

text:
Complete Helpline Directory — Property and Land Disputes in UP

ONLINE PORTALS (Available 24 hours, free):
upbhulekh.gov.in — Check Khatauni, Khasra, Real-Time Record of Rights, Dispute Status, Sale Status of plots. Contact: 0522-2217145, bhulekh-up@gov.in
upbhunaksha.gov.in — Bhu-Naksha (digital boundary map for all plots in UP)
igrsup.gov.in — Stamp duty payment, property registration status, certified copy requests, registry office details
up-rera.in — File RERA complaints, check builder registration, project details, complaint status
boardofrevenue.up.nic.in — Board of Revenue UP — highest revenue authority, second appeal information

PHONE HELPLINES:
1076 — CM Helpline UP: For government official inaction — use when Tahsildar, SDO, or any revenue officer is not acting on your complaint. Complaints are tracked and followed up.
0522-2209457 — UP State Legal Services Authority (UPSLSA): Free legal representation for property disputes. Cannot afford a lawyer? Call here. District offices in every jila.
15100 — NALSA (National Legal Services Authority): Free legal aid nationwide.
1800-103-0025 — Income Tax Department: Report suspected benami property.
1800-11-6163 — Central RERA Helpline: For general RERA Act queries.

OFFICES TO VISIT:
Tahsildar's office — For: mutation application, certified copy of Khatauni, encroachment complaint, land record correction. Every tehsil has one.
Sub-Registrar's office — For: property registration (sale deed, gift deed, lease deed). igrsup.gov.in has district-wise office locations.
SDO (Sub-Divisional Officer) — For: boundary disputes, appeal of Tahsildar's mutation orders.
UP RERA Office — Lucknow (main office) and Greater Noida (NCR Bench): For submission of physical complaint copies after online filing.
Board of Revenue, Lucknow — For second appeals in land disputes on questions of law.

All portals listed are free to access. All helpline numbers are toll-free from any phone.

metadata.source: UP Revenue Department; UP RERA; IGRSUP; Board of Revenue UP; UPSLSA; NALSA 2025
